main_damage_causes: Systematic removal of Hagrid's emotional depth and vulnerability — particularly his half-giant arc in PoA, his confession about his mother in GoF, and his extended grief scenes — reduces him from a complex figure navigating prejudice and loss to a warm but essentially comic supporting character

Compression of his role as Harry's primary moral educator and emotional anchor in the early films: the extended conversations in the hut, on the train, and in Diagon Alley that establish him as Harry's first real guide to both the wizarding world and to his own identity are cut, making the relationship feel assumed rather than earned

The giant mission arc in GoF/OotP — Hagrid's most significant act of adult agency in the war — is reduced to near-nothing, meaning the films never show him as a genuine soldier in the Order of the Phoenix, only as a gamekeeper who occasionally helps

damaging_scenes.scene: PS/SS Film - Privet Drive delivery scene
damaging_scenes.book_baseline: Hagrid arrives emotionally devastated, sobbing loudly over Lily and James, asks to say goodbye to Harry, kisses him, howls like a wounded dog. McGonagall has to shush him. He stands watching with shaking shoulders as Dumbledore places Harry on the doorstep. His grief is raw, prolonged, and deeply characterizing.
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: Hagrid hands the baby over quickly, sniffles briefly, clears his throat, and nods. The extended grief scene, the goodbye kiss, the howling, and the emotional collapse are entirely absent. He is a delivery mechanism.
damaging_scenes.explanation: The book establishes from page one that Hagrid loves Harry with overwhelming, unselfconscious emotion. Cutting his grief removes the first and most defining demonstration of his character: that he feels everything at full volume and cannot contain it.

damaging_scenes.scene: PS/SS Film - Hut on the Rock arrival
damaging_scenes.book_baseline: Hagrid is commanding and funny on arrival: bends the gun barrel, calls Vernon a 'great prune', makes fire, cooks sausages, takes charge of the situation with confident authority. He is also the one who delivers the full Voldemort backstory to Harry with emotional weight, pausing to blow his nose when speaking of Lily and James.
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: The practical competence and humor are mostly retained, but the Voldemort backstory is compressed into a brief pub scene (Leaky Cauldron) rather than the hut. The emotional weight of Hagrid telling Harry about his parents' deaths — pausing, crying, calling them 'the nicest people you could find' — is largely stripped out.
damaging_scenes.explanation: The book's hut scene is the foundation of the Harry-Hagrid relationship: Hagrid is the first person to tell Harry the truth about his parents and to grieve with him. Moving and compressing this loses the intimacy of that first bond.

damaging_scenes.scene: PS/SS Film - Leaky Cauldron / Diagon Alley
damaging_scenes.book_baseline: Hagrid is proud, knowledgeable, and socially at ease in the wizarding world. He explains Quidditch, defends Hermione's Muggle-born status (not yet met), defends Harry against Malfoy's implied snobbery, buys Hedwig as a birthday gift with gruff affection, and shows genuine excitement at Harry's wonder.
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: Hagrid is present and functional but the extended conversations — about Quidditch, about Slytherin, about Muggle-borns, about Harry's parents being 'head boy and girl' — are cut. His role is reduced to guide and key-holder.
damaging_scenes.explanation: These conversations establish Hagrid as Harry's first real educator about the wizarding world and as someone with strong moral views. Their absence makes him a tour guide rather than a mentor.

damaging_scenes.scene: PS/SS Film - Hagrid's slip about Fluffy / Nicolas Flamel
damaging_scenes.book_baseline: Hagrid's accidental revelation of Flamel's name is a character beat showing his inability to keep secrets when emotionally invested — a consistent and important flaw. He is furious with himself immediately.
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: The slip is present and handled reasonably well. This is one of the more faithful moments.
damaging_scenes.explanation: Mostly faithful; minor compression only. Low damage.

damaging_scenes.scene: PS/SS Film - Norbert hatching scene
damaging_scenes.book_baseline: Hagrid's dragon obsession is portrayed as a deep, irrational, and somewhat dangerous passion. He names the dragon, sings it lullabies, calls himself 'Mummy', and is genuinely heartbroken when Norbert must go. Ron notes he treats it 'like a fluffy little bunny rabbit' even as it bites him.
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: The hatching is shown and Hagrid's affection is present, but the extended absurdity — the lullabies, the teddy bear, the weeks of Ron feeding it dead rats, Hagrid's complete inability to see the danger — is compressed. The comedy and the pathos of his farewell are reduced.
damaging_scenes.explanation: The Norbert arc is one of the clearest illustrations of Hagrid's defining flaw: his love for dangerous creatures overrides all practical judgment. Compression softens this.

damaging_scenes.scene: PS/SS Film - Forbidden Forest detention
damaging_scenes.book_baseline: Hagrid is authoritative with the students, stands up to Filch, organizes the groups strategically, shows genuine expertise about forest creatures, and demonstrates real courage when he hears something dangerous. He is protective and competent.
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: Hagrid's competence and authority in the forest are present. The scene is reasonably faithful in broad strokes, though some of his dialogue establishing forest knowledge is cut.
damaging_scenes.explanation: Mostly faithful. Minor losses in establishing his expertise.

damaging_scenes.scene: PS/SS Film - Hagrid reveals Fluffy's weakness (music)
damaging_scenes.book_baseline: Hagrid is manipulated by a disguised Quirrell/Voldemort into revealing how to get past Fluffy, having been plied with drinks. This is a significant plot point showing his vulnerability to flattery and drink, and his inability to see he's being used. He is devastated when he realizes.
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: The scene where Harry questions Hagrid about the stranger is present and the revelation is handled. Hagrid's self-recrimination in the hospital wing ('It's all my ruddy fault!') is present in the film.
damaging_scenes.explanation: Reasonably faithful. The key beats of his guilt and vulnerability are retained.

damaging_scenes.scene: PS/SS Film - End of year: photo album gift
damaging_scenes.book_baseline: Hagrid gives Harry the photo album having spent time writing to all his parents' old school friends. It is an act of profound thoughtfulness and love — he knew Harry had no photos and quietly fixed that. Harry cannot speak.
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: The gift is present and the emotional beat is retained. This is one of the more faithful moments in the film.
damaging_scenes.explanation: Mostly faithful. The scene works.

damaging_scenes.scene: CoS Film - Hagrid's expulsion backstory / Tom Riddle diary
damaging_scenes.book_baseline: The diary memory shows young Hagrid as naive and trusting, genuinely believing Aragog is innocent, trying to protect his friend. Riddle's manipulation of him is chilling precisely because Hagrid is so guileless. The scene establishes that Hagrid's love for creatures has always made him exploitable.
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: The memory scene is present and young Hagrid's defense of Aragog is shown. The scene is reasonably faithful.
damaging_scenes.explanation: Mostly faithful in the memory sequence.

damaging_scenes.scene: CoS Film - Hagrid's arrest / Azkaban
damaging_scenes.book_baseline: Hagrid's speech before being taken — 'If anyone wanted ter find out some stuff, all they'd have ter do is follow the spiders' — is a deliberate, coded message to Harry and Ron, showing that even under extreme duress he is trying to help them. His defiance toward Lucius Malfoy ('Yeh can take Dumbledore! Take him away an' the Muggle-borns won't stand a chance!') shows moral courage.
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: The arrest scene is present. Hagrid's coded spider message is retained. His defense of Dumbledore is present. This is one of the more faithful scenes.
damaging_scenes.explanation: Mostly faithful.

damaging_scenes.scene: PoA Film - Hippogriff lesson
damaging_scenes.book_baseline: Hagrid's first lesson is genuinely exciting and well-organized. He is nervous but competent, and Harry's success with Buckbeak is a real triumph for both of them. When Malfoy is injured, Hagrid is shaken but not incompetent — he acts immediately and correctly. His subsequent depression (drinking, self-doubt) is a realistic response to the threat to his job.
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: The lesson is well-handled and Hagrid's enthusiasm and nervousness are present. The Buckbeak flight is expanded into a spectacular sequence. Hagrid's post-incident depression is shown. This is one of the better Hagrid scenes in the films.
damaging_scenes.explanation: Largely faithful and well-executed.

damaging_scenes.scene: PoA Film - Hagrid mediates Ron/Hermione conflict
damaging_scenes.book_baseline: In the book, Hagrid's intervention in the Ron/Hermione conflict over Scabbers and Crookshanks is a significant character moment: he lectures them about valuing friendship over pets, tells them Hermione has been helping with Buckbeak's case while they ignored it, and makes them feel genuinely ashamed. It shows his emotional intelligence and his role as a moral anchor for the trio.
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: The film has Hagrid engineer an awkward hug between Ron and Hermione and facilitate a handshake between Harry and Ron. The substance of his lecture — his disappointment, his point about Hermione's loyalty, his moral authority — is replaced with physical comedy.
damaging_scenes.explanation: This scene is one of the clearest demonstrations in the books that Hagrid has genuine wisdom and emotional intelligence beneath his bluster. The film turns it into a sight gag, reducing him to comic relief at the exact moment the books use him as a moral voice.

damaging_scenes.scene: PoA Film - Hagrid's half-giant revelation / Madame Maxime
damaging_scenes.book_baseline: Hagrid's confession to Madame Maxime about his giantess mother is one of the most vulnerable moments in the series. He speaks of his father with love, of his mother's abandonment without self-pity, and of Dumbledore's kindness. His subsequent shame when the Daily Prophet exposes him, and his hiding in his hut, show the real cost of prejudice against half-breeds.
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: This entire arc — the overheard conversation, the Rita Skeeter article, Hagrid hiding in his hut for weeks, Dumbledore visiting him, the trio's efforts to bring him back — does not occur in the PoA film. The half-giant revelation is absent entirely from the third film.
damaging_scenes.explanation: This is the most significant arc cut for Hagrid across the films. His half-giant identity, the prejudice he faces, his shame and self-doubt, and his eventual reclamation of dignity ('I am what I am, an' I'm not ashamed') constitute a complete emotional arc in PoA. Its absence means the films never explore the theme of blood prejudice through Hagrid's personal experience, and his character never achieves this particular depth.

damaging_scenes.scene: GoF Film - Hagrid shows Harry the dragons
damaging_scenes.book_baseline: Hagrid's decision to show Harry the dragons is motivated by genuine love and protectiveness — he cannot bear the thought of Harry going into the task blind. He also brings Madame Maxime, which is a romantic gesture that inadvertently tips off Karkaroff. His motivations are complex: love for Harry, romantic feelings for Maxime, and his characteristic inability to think through consequences.
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: The scene is present and Hagrid's motivation (showing Harry the dragons out of love) is clear. The romantic subplot with Madame Maxime is present. This is reasonably faithful.
damaging_scenes.explanation: Mostly faithful.

damaging_scenes.scene: GoF Film - Hagrid's giant mission / absence from school
damaging_scenes.book_baseline: Hagrid's mission to the giants is a significant arc: he is gone for months, returns badly injured, describes the mission in detail (the Gurg, Golgomath, the Death Eaters' counter-mission, Madame Maxime's courage), and the whole episode establishes him as an active member of the Order doing dangerous work. His injuries from Grawp are also revealed here.
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: Hagrid's absence from school is noted but the giant mission is reduced to a brief conversation. The detail of the mission — the political complexity, his injuries, Madame Maxime's role, the failure — is almost entirely cut. His return is a minor scene.
damaging_scenes.explanation: The giant mission is Hagrid's most significant act of agency in the series outside of his role as Harry's guide. It shows him as a genuine soldier in the war, not just a gamekeeper. Its compression to near-nothing removes his most adult and consequential action.

damaging_scenes.scene: OotP Film - Hagrid's Care of Magical Creatures lessons / Umbridge inspection
damaging_scenes.book_baseline: Hagrid's lessons under Umbridge's inspection are a sustained arc showing his loss of confidence, his retreat to flobberworms, his distraction and jumpiness, and Umbridge's deliberate humiliation of him (speaking loudly and slowly as if he cannot understand normal English, asking students if they can understand him). The arc culminates in his being put on probation.
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: The Thestral lesson is shown and Umbridge's inspection is present, including her condescending behavior. However, the extended arc of Hagrid's declining confidence across multiple lessons, the flobberworm period, and the full weight of his probation are compressed.
damaging_scenes.explanation: The film captures the key beats but loses the sustained portrait of a good person being ground down by institutional prejudice. The arc is present in outline but not in emotional depth.

damaging_scenes.scene: OotP Film - Grawp subplot
damaging_scenes.book_baseline: The Grawp subplot is extensive: Hagrid's love for his half-brother, his delusional belief that Grawp can be civilized, the injuries he sustains, the centaurs' anger, Firenze's warning, and the trio's reluctant promise to look after Grawp. It is simultaneously comic and tragic — Hagrid's capacity for love extending even to a creature that keeps hitting him.
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: Grawp is present and the basic situation is established. However, the full absurdity and pathos of the subplot — Hagrid's injuries, the centaur conflict, Firenze's warning, the trio's horror at being asked to 'teach' a giant — is compressed. Grawp functions mainly as a plot device in the battle.
damaging_scenes.explanation: The subplot is present but thinned. The comedy and the pathos of Hagrid's relationship with Grawp are reduced.

damaging_scenes.scene: OotP Film - Hagrid's expulsion from Hogwarts
damaging_scenes.book_baseline: Hagrid's expulsion from Hogwarts by Umbridge — with four Aurors attempting to Stun him, his giant blood making him resistant, him knocking them out, carrying Fang, and fleeing into the night — is a dramatic and moving scene. McGonagall is Stunned trying to intervene. Harry watches from the Astronomy Tower during his OWL exam.
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: The scene is present and well-executed. Hagrid's resistance to Stunning Spells, his fury, and his escape are shown. McGonagall's injury is included. This is one of the more faithful and effective Hagrid scenes in the films.
damaging_scenes.explanation: Mostly faithful and dramatically effective.

damaging_scenes.scene: HBP Film - Aragog's funeral / Slughorn memory
damaging_scenes.book_baseline: The Aragog funeral scene is one of the most tonally complex Hagrid moments: he is genuinely grief-stricken over a spider that tried to eat his friends, and his eulogy request ('He'd've liked that') is both funny and touching. His relationship with Aragog — spanning decades, built on loyalty — is a microcosm of his entire character.
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: The scene is present and handled with reasonable fidelity. Hagrid's grief is shown, Slughorn's eulogy is present, and the tonal complexity is mostly preserved.
damaging_scenes.explanation: Mostly faithful.

damaging_scenes.scene: DH1 Film - Seven Potters / motorbike chase
damaging_scenes.book_baseline: Hagrid's role in the Seven Potters sequence is to protect Harry on the motorbike, using Arthur's modifications (dragon fire, nets) and his own physical toughness. His terror for Harry, his determination, and his grief over Hedwig are all present. He is active and brave throughout.
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: The sequence is present and Hagrid's role is largely faithful. His bravery, his use of the bike's modifications, and his concern for Harry are all shown. This is one of the better Hagrid sequences in the later films.
damaging_scenes.explanation: Mostly faithful.

damaging_scenes.scene: DH2 Film - Hagrid carrying Harry's body
damaging_scenes.book_baseline: Hagrid carries Harry's apparently dead body from the forest to the castle, sobbing blindly, unable to see where he is going. His grief is total and uncontained. When Harry is revealed alive, Hagrid's joy is equally overwhelming. The scene is the culmination of their relationship across seven books.
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: The scene is present and emotionally effective. Hagrid's grief while carrying Harry is shown. His cry of 'HARRY! NO! WHAT'RE YEH DOIN' 'ERE!' when Harry steps forward is present. The reunion is handled with appropriate emotion.
damaging_scenes.explanation: Mostly faithful and emotionally resonant.

damaging_scenes.scene: Multiple films - Hagrid's voice/dialect consistency
damaging_scenes.book_baseline: Hagrid's dialect in the books is consistent and specific: dropped h's, 'yeh' for 'you', 'ter' for 'to', 'o' course', 'blimey', 'codswallop'. His speech patterns are part of his characterization — working-class, uneducated but not stupid, warm and direct.
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: Robbie Coltrane's performance captures the dialect well and is largely faithful to the books' rendering of Hagrid's voice. This is not a significant area of damage.
damaging_scenes.explanation: The casting and performance are strong. Minimal damage here.

damaging_scenes.scene: PoA Film - Buckbeak execution / time-turner sequence
damaging_scenes.book_baseline: During the time-turner sequence, Hagrid's emotional state when Buckbeak is apparently executed — his howl of grief, then his joy when Buckbeak has 'escaped' — is a key emotional beat. His 'Bless his little beak, he's gone! Musta pulled himself free!' is one of the most joyful moments in the book.
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: The sequence is present and Hagrid's grief and subsequent joy are shown. The line about Buckbeak pulling himself free is present. This is handled well.
damaging_scenes.explanation: Mostly faithful.

damaging_scenes.scene: GoF Film - Hagrid's relationship with Madame Maxime
damaging_scenes.book_baseline: Hagrid's courtship of Madame Maxime is tender, awkward, and revealing: he dresses up, uses cologne, tries to comb his hair, and is genuinely vulnerable. His confession about his mother — 'She wasn't really the maternal sort... it's not in their natures, is it?' — is one of the most unguarded moments in the series. Maxime's rejection when he names her as half-giant is painful.
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: The romantic subplot is present and some of the awkward courtship details (the suit, the flower) are shown. However, the overheard conversation where Hagrid confesses about his mother — the most emotionally significant part — is cut. The rejection and its aftermath are absent.
damaging_scenes.explanation: The confession about his mother is the only time in the series Hagrid speaks at length about his childhood and his feelings of abandonment. Its absence removes a layer of depth from his character that the films never recover.
